description: THREE-WAY trigger discrimination for 'instrumentation': USER-facing PRODUCT analytics (this skill) vs SYSTEM telemetry for engineers (observability-operator) vs the SKILL-LIBRARY usage signal (skill-usage-instrumenter); plus the schema-vs-firing seam with event-schema-architect.

cases.prompt: Instrument our product analytics events: decide client vs server capture, set identity at capture, gate on consent, prevent double-counting, and QA that events fire per the tracking plan.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary, three-way) — capturing user-behavior events with consent, de-dup, and tracking QA is product-analytics instrumentation. Not engineer telemetry, not library usage.
cases.reason: Firing and QAing user-behavior analytics events is this skill's core scope, distinct from system telemetry and from skill-usage measurement.

cases.prompt: Instrument our services with structured logs, metrics, and traces; wire health checks; and set up alerts and dashboards for on-call engineers.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (three-way) — logs/metrics/traces, health checks, and alert/dashboard config are engineer-facing SYSTEM telemetry for operating the stack.
cases.reason: observability-operator operates the observability stack for engineers. This skill instruments USER behavior for product/growth decisions.

cases.prompt: We want to know which skills in our skills library actually fire in practice, and which never do, without capturing prompt content.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (three-way) — measuring which library SKILLS fire, under strict minimization (skill names/enums only), is the library's self-measurement.
cases.reason: skill-usage-instrumenter designs the skill-library usage-evidence layer. This skill instruments end-user product behavior, a different object and privacy stance.

cases.prompt: Define the analytics events themselves: names, property schemas, the identity model, and the tracking plan.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (within pack) — defining events/properties/identity is the schema, upstream of firing them.
cases.reason: event-schema-architect defines what events exist. This skill decides where/how they fire; it instruments against that schema.
